<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249565583 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 05679: Missing data in diagrams, matrices, tables, and relation maps

- page_id: `249565583`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249565583/Missing+data+in+diagrams+matrices+tables+and+relation+maps
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-08-18T16:31:37.991+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > UAF 1.3 modeling > Migration User Guides > UPDM projects migration manual > UPDM 2.1 migration to UAF 1.1 > Mapping issues and solutions
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Since UPDM 2 profile [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Removed elements' space=''], the Element Type of matrices, tables, and relation maps becomes empty after the migration. The same goes for the customized elements derived from the removed UPDM2 elements. If the UPDM2 [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Replaced and renamed elements' space=''] to the UAF element, the UAF element is set as an Element Type (criteria).

#### NOTE: Exception

Exception

If Operational Event Trace, Resource Event Trace, Service Interaction was set as an Element Type criteria, after the migration an Interaction replaces those elements.

After the migration from UPDM 2 to UAF, some tags are no longer supported. During the migration process, all the information from those tags is moved to the**To Do**property of the tag owner. To find this property, open the appropriate element's Specification window. You can display the tag information in the note, anchored to the element.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Removed tag added to ToDo property

Condition::conditionKind tag was removed, so after the migration it will be added into ToDo property in the Specification window of the Condition element.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><span>Since UPDM 2 profile <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Removed elements" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[elements were removed]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, the Element Type of matrices, tables, and relation maps becomes empty after the migration. The same goes for the customized elements derived from the removed UPDM2 elements. If the UPDM2 <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Replaced and renamed elements" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[element is migrated]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> to the UAF element, the UAF element is set as an Element Type (criteria).</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4f830871-f7d3-43f9-a110-feeded1958ed"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Exception</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><span>If Operational Event Trace, <span>Resource Event Trace, <span>Service Interaction was set as an Element Type criteria, after the migration an Interaction replaces those elements.</span></span></span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">After the migration from UPDM 2 to UAF, some tags are no longer supported. During the migration process, all the information from those tags is moved to the </span><strong>To Do</strong><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> property of the tag owner. To find this property, open the appropriate element's Specification window. You can display the tag information in the note, anchored to the element.</span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="migration_tagged_values.png" /></ac:image></span></p><h3>Removed tag added to ToDo property</h3><p>Condition::conditionKind tag was removed, so after the migration it will be added into ToDo property in the Specification window of the Condition element.</p><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"><br /></span></p>
````

The same goes for the customized elements derived from the removed UPDM2 elements. If the UPDM2 [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Replaced and renamed elements' space=''] to the UAF element, the UAF element is set as an Element Type (criteria).

After the migration from UPDM 2 to UAF, some tags are no longer supported. During the migration process, all the information from those tags is moved to the**To Do**property of the tag owner. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243973864 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 05681: Missing expression language

- page_id: `243973864`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243973864/Missing+expression+language
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T19:18:22.930+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Macro engine > Supported scripting languages
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The modeling tool supports multiple [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Supported scripting languages' space=''] you can use for expression specification. Additionally, you can [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Supported scripting languages' space=''] you may need. However, if your project contains expressions that use a scripting language that is neither built-in nor manually added to the tool, it is considered missing in the tool; thus, the specified expressions cannot be executed, causing errors in the project.

##### Expression evaluation failure

For example, if you open a table containing a custom column that has an expression with a JavaScript Nashorn script, an 'Expression evaluation failure' error notification is displayed. Since, in this case, JavaScript Nashorn is neither built-in nor added manually to the modeling tool, the script cannot be executed, causing an evaluation error, as shown below.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

The error notification indicates the element containing the failed expression. Clicking the icon on the bottom right opens a Notification Window that displays the element name with the script that cannot be executed.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']Expression evaluation failure notification in the Notification Window

The missing language is also indicated in the Expression dialog's Language field for scripts.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']Missing language indication in the Expression dialog

##### Resolving expression evaluation failure

To resolve an expression evaluation failure, do one of the following

- Rewrite the script in a scripting language that is already supported by the modeling tool. To learn more about the built-in languages, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Supported scripting languages' space=''] page.
- Add the missing language to the modeling tool. For more information, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Adding a scripting language to the modeling tool' space=''] page.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>The modeling tool supports multiple <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Supported scripting languages" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[built-in scripting languages]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> you can use for expression specification. Additionally, you can <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Supported scripting languages" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[add other scripting languages]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> you may need. However, if your project contains expressions that use a scripting language that is neither built-in nor manually added to the tool, it is considered missing in the tool; thus, the specified expressions cannot be executed, causing errors in the project.</p><h3>Expression evaluation failure</h3><p>For example, if you open a table containing a custom column that has an expression with a JavaScript Nashorn script, an 'Expression evaluation failure' error notification is displayed. Since, in this case, JavaScript Nashorn is neither built-in nor added manually to the modeling tool, the script cannot be executed, causing an evaluation error, as shown below. </p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="expression_evaluation_error.png" /></ac:image> </h6><p>The error notification indicates the <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">element containing the failed expression</span>. Clicking the icon on the bottom right opens a Notification Window that displays the element name with the script that cannot be executed. </p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="notification_window_expression_evaluation_error.png" /></ac:image>Expression evaluation failure notification in the Notification Window</h6><p>The missing language is also indicated in the Expression dialog's Language field for scripts.</p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="missing_language_expression_dialog.png" /></ac:image>Missing language indication in the Expression dialog</h6><h3>Resolving expression evaluation failure</h3><p>To resolve an expression evaluation failure, do one of the following</p><hr /><ul><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Rewrite the script in a scripting language that is already supported by the modeling tool.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6ea17ebc-15ef-460f-b0f5-cbbe38882859"><ac:rich-text-body><p>To learn more about the built-in languages, see the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Supported scripting languages" /></ac:link> page.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Add the missing language to the modeling tool. </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="de6f0ee7-9c62-4a58-b0b8-c4c1f29387c3"><ac:rich-text-body><p>For more information, see the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Adding a scripting language to the modeling tool" /></ac:link> page. </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ul>
````

## PAGE 05682: Mission

- page_id: `249566848`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249566848/Mission

- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1487246060

1487246062

1487246061

**Description**

A Mission indicates the ongoing operational Activity of the enterprise. The Mission describes what the business is or will be doing on a day-to-day basis.

A Mission makes a Vision operative. It indicates the ongoing Activity that makes the Vision a reality. A Mission is planned by means of Strategies.

**Notation**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

1487246059

**Related elements**

- Means Concepts
- Mean Concept Relationships

**Related diagram**

- Business Motivation Diagram

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249562855 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 05683: MODAF

- page_id: `249562855`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249562855/MODAF
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-08-18T13:09:24.628+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > UAF 1.3 modeling
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

According to modeling needs, there are the following MODAF templates for different purposes:

- **MODAF Project**- creating a MODAF project will switch the application to the MODAF graphical user interface and use the MODAF model templates.
- **MODAF Project Template with Process Guide**- using this template, you will get a new MODAF project enhanced with a modeling guide. The modeling guide helps you to build the architecture by providing summary information about the MODAF structure and the architecture creation process.

All MODAF views are described in the following sections:

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

The modeling guide helps you to build the architecture by providing summary information about the MODAF structure and the architecture creation process.</span></li></ul><p>All MODAF views are described in the following sections:</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="a00715aa-d17f-4574-86dd-b775749c82d7" /></p><p><br /></p>

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c85f1e71-e10f-4a6e-8b5c-c4c4cb029dba"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">413330060</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="92a5557d-0e58-40f9-9b56-8098879b1b7c"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">413330063</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="74c76553-55d6-4319-8739-6506458aae75"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">413330061</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>The AcquisitionElements describe project details, including dependencies between projects and capability integration. These Views guide the acquisition and fielding processes.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6b3cd7d7-b652-4747-8531-93ffe1cebe4e"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">413330058</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Views</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="c91c3b74-a8a6-4390-9a57-4ee0af500ff0" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249562857 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 05685: MODAF All Views Viewpoint

- page_id: `249562857`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249562857/MODAF+All+Views+Viewpoint
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-08-18T13:09:24.666+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > UAF 1.3 modeling > MODAF
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

411478012

411478014

411478013

Elements that are part of the All View. The All-Views (AVs) provide an overarching description of the architecture, its scope, ownership, timeframe and all of the other meta data that is required in order to effectively search and query architectural models. They also provide a place to record any findings arising from the architecting process. The AVs include a dictionary of the terms used in the construction of the architecture – which helps others fully understand its meaning at a later date. Since the AVs provide critical information for the future access and exploitation of an architectural model their population is essential whenever an architecture is created or modified. The AVs provide a critical input into the processes that provide architectural governance.

411478011

**Views**

****

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

The All-Views (AVs) provide an overarching description of the architecture, its scope, ownership, timeframe and all of the other meta data that is required in order to effectively search and query architectural models. They also provide a place to record any findings arising from the architecting process. The AVs include a dictionary of the terms used in the construction of the architecture – which helps others fully understand its meaning at a later date. Since the AVs provide critical information for the future access and exploitation of an architectural model their population is essential whenever an architecture is created or modified. The AVs provide a critical input into the processes that provide architectural governance.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ddb43cbf-1af3-4151-8538-99e7bf33980b"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">411478011</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Views</strong></p><p><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="a3f29e67-0ea5-4b50-b4d6-ff0d3a514e84" /></strong></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249562923 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 05686: MODAF Operational Viewpoint

- page_id: `249562923`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249562923/MODAF+Operational+Viewpoint
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-08-18T13:09:26.433+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > UAF 1.3 modeling > MODAF
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

413846051

413846053

413846052

The Operational Viewpoint is about real-world activities, the people and machinery that perform them, and the means by which they are performed. The Operational Viewpoint is divided into nine views intended to answer the “who”, “what”, “when”, “where”, “why”, and “how” of a mission.

The Operational Views are common to MODAF and DoDAF; OV-1b, OV-1c, and OV-2 however, have been customized to provide for MOD requirements. The Operational Views describe the tasks and activities, operational elements, and information exchanges required to conduct operations. In MODAF thinking, the OV Views are considered to illustrate the Logical Architecture of the enterprise.

413846050

**View**

****

In MODAF thinking, the OV Views are considered to illustrate the Logical Architecture of the enterprise.</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="widget" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="51021151-7b24-4f28-ab31-f3d730d0b90f"><ac:parameter ac:name="url"><ri:url ri:value="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jrpPuZ8Vf74" /></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b8cbd928-d9b8-44d5-a428-f1c17ff35954"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">413846050</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>View</strong></p><p><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="7c989114-0d1c-4195-a04c-eabf736fc9cc" /></strong></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

417346696

417346698

417346697

The Services View (SOV) shows the elements that are part of SOV-1 through SOV-5.

The Service-Orientated View is a description of services needed to directly support the operational domain as described in the Operational View. A service is described as a unit of work through which a particular Resource provides a useful result to a consuming Resource.

417346695

## PAGE 05688: MODAF Strategic Viewpoint

- page_id: `249562866`
- space_key: `MT`

- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

411757999

411758001

411758000

The Strategic View (StV) shows the elements that are part of StV-1 through StV-6. The Strategic Elements are used in the Strategic View that provides an overall Enterprise Architecture assessment of the Capabilities and their relationships facilitating Capability Management (e.g. capability introduction, integration, re-alignment and removal). While an Enterprise will have a number of UAF Architecture Descriptions that have the Operational, System, Technical Standards, and All Views, only one Strategic View will exist across a number of Architecture Descriptions.

411757997

**Views**

## PAGE 05689: MODAF Systems Viewpoint

- page_id: `249563018`
- space_key: `MT`

419208934

419208936

419208935

The System View (SV) shows the elements that are part of SV-1 through SV-12. Models in the System Viewpoint represent alternate realizations in terms of equipment capability of the operational capabilities expressed through models in the Operational Viewpoint and in the User Requirements. The System Viewpoint primarily addresses the specification of the system capability needed (rather than implementation details). Significant changes originally made in MODAF improved the ability for modelers to represent configuration of capability that include people as well as systems and platforms.

419208933

**Views**

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249563099 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 05690: MODAF Technical Standards Viewpoint

- page_id: `249563099`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249563099/MODAF+Technical+Standards+Viewpoint
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-08-18T13:09:31.649+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > UAF 1.3 modeling > MODAF
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

418729522

418729524

418729523

The Technical View (TV) shows the elements that are part of TV-1 through TV-2.

The Technical View is a set of views delineating standards, rules, notations, and conventions that apply to the implementation of the system architecture. When the standards profile is tied to the system elements to which they apply, TV-1 serves as the bridge between the SV and TV. SV-9 forecasts relate to the TV-1 in that a timed technology forecast may contribute to the decision to retire or phase out the use of a certain standard in connection with a system element. Similarly, SV-9 forecasts relate to TV-2 standards forecasts in that a certain standard may be adopted depending on a certain technology becoming available (e.g., the availability of Java Script may influence the decision to adopt a new HTML standard).

MODAF extends the core DoDAF Technical Standards Views to include non-technical standards and policies applicable to the architecture such as operational doctrine, industry process standards, etc. Additionally, the TV-1 may also document policies and standards applicable to the operational or business context. MODAF also distinguishes between ‘applicability’ and ‘conformance’ with regard to architectural elements. If a standard is applicable to a given architecture, that architecture need not be fully conformant with the standard. The degree of conformance to a given standard may be judged on a risk basis at an approval point. An association between a Standard and an architectural element is not to be interpreted as stating the level of compliance of the element is fully compliant with that Standard. Additional evidences would need to be given (outside MODAF) to confirm the level of compliance. Finally, MODAF adds the explicit requirement that any Standards cited in TV-1 View must, where appropriate, be in accordance with the trend towards open architectures – i.e. standards which encourage stove-piped systems are expressly prohibited.

418729521

**Views**

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="24d14305-3e75-4bd1-b841-4581d318f48f"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">418729522</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="bb0ba76b-4983-43c4-85b7-1a23b08e652d"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">418729524</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ab7dcaaf-de79-467a-a5ae-4ac55bbd6721"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">418729523</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>The Technical View (TV) shows the elements that are part of TV-1 through TV-2.</p><p>The Technical View is a set of views delineating standards, rules, notations, and conventions that apply to the implementation of the system architecture. When the standards profile is tied to the system elements to which they apply, TV-1 serves as the bridge between the SV and TV. SV-9 forecasts relate to the TV-1 in that a timed technology forecast may contribute to the decision to retire or phase out the use of a certain standard in connection with a system element. Similarly, SV-9 forecasts relate to TV-2 standards forecasts in that a certain standard may be adopted depending on a certain technology becoming available (e.g., the availability of Java Script may influence the decision to adopt a new HTML standard).</p><p>MODAF extends the core DoDAF Technical Standards Views to include non-technical standards and policies applicable to the architecture such as operational doctrine, industry process standards, etc. Additionally, the TV-1 may also document policies and standards applicable to the operational or business context. MODAF also distinguishes between ‘applicability’ and ‘conformance’ with regard to architectural elements. If a standard is applicable to a given architecture, that architecture need not be fully conformant with the standard. The degree of conformance to a given standard may be judged on a risk basis at an approval point. An association between a Standard and an architectural element is not to be interpreted as stating the level of compliance of the element is fully compliant with that Standard. Additional evidences would need to be given (outside MODAF) to confirm the level of compliance. Finally, MODAF adds the explicit requirement that any Standards cited in TV-1 View must, where appropriate, be in accordance with the trend towards open architectures – i.e. standards which encourage stove-piped systems are expressly prohibited.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="eab04b60-7f6b-4780-b0ad-0b6684437e20"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">418729521</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Views</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="256e754f-3204-47a1-bb84-748b710aaf8c" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249562910 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 05691: MODAF. AcV-1 Acquisition Clusters

- page_id: `249562910`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249562910/MODAF.+AcV-1+Acquisition+Clusters
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-08-18T13:09:26.108+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > UAF 1.3 modeling > MODAF > MODAF Acquisition Viewpoint
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

413387346

413387349

413387348

**Description**

The Acquisition Clusters (AcV-1) View describes how acquisition projects are grouped in organisational terms as a coherent portfolio of acquisition programmes.

The AcV-1 View provides a way of describing the organizational relationships between multiple acquisition projects, each of which is responsible for delivering individual systems or capabilities. By definition, this View covers acquisition programmes consisting of multiple projects and will generally not be developed by those building Architectures for an individual project. In essence, the AcV-1 is an organizational breakdown consisting of actual organizations (see OV-4). The view is strongly linked with the StV-4 which shows capability clusters and dependencies.

The AcV-1 View is hierarchical in nature. Higher level groupings of projects (or, rather the organisations that own these projects) form acquisition clusters.

The intent of an AcV-1 View Product is to show:

- All of the acquisition projects delivering systems or system of systems (SoS) within the acquisition programmes under consideration.
- Other systems and SoS which may have a bearing on the Architecture.
- How the systems will be best integrated into acquisition clusters.
- The nesting of acquisition clusters to form a hierarchy.

**Implementation**

AcV-1 can be represented using:

- AcV-1 Acquisition Clusters diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.
- AcV-1 Responsibility Matrix which is an editable Dependency Matrix.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### AcV-1 Responsibility Matrix

**Related views**

In essence, the AcV-1 is an organizational breakdown consisting of actual organizations (see OV-4). The view is strongly linked with the StV-4 which shows capability clusters and dependencies.

413387345

By definition, this View covers acquisition programmes consisting of multiple projects and will generally not be developed by those building Architectures for an individual project. In essence, the AcV-1 is an organizational breakdown consisting of actual organizations (see OV-4). The view is strongly linked with the StV-4 which shows capability clusters and dependencies.</p><p>The AcV-1 View is hierarchical in nature. Higher level groupings of projects (or, rather the organisations that own these projects) form acquisition clusters.</p><p>The intent of an AcV-1 View Product is to show:</p><ul><li>All of the acquisition projects delivering systems or system of systems (SoS) within the acquisition programmes under consideration.</li><li>Other systems and SoS which may have a bearing on the Architecture.</li><li>How the systems will be best integrated into acquisition clusters.</li><li>The nesting of acquisition clusters to form a hierarchy.</li></ul><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>AcV-1 can be represented using:</p><ul><li>AcV-1 Acquisition Clusters diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.</li><li>AcV-1 Responsibility Matrix which is an editable Dependency Matrix.</li></ul><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Acv_1.jpg" /></ac:image></p><h6>AcV-1 Responsibility Matrix</h6><p><strong>Related views</strong></p><p>In essence, the AcV-1 is an organizational breakdown consisting of actual organizations (see OV-4). The view is strongly linked with the StV-4 which shows capability clusters and dependencies.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="44dedf88-c1f1-4a3b-8275-806a92a0fde4"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">413387345</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related elements</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Actual Organization" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Actual Post" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Actual Project" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Responsible For" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Project Sequence" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Project" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Project Milestone" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Project Milestone Role" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Actual Project Milestone Role" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Status Indicators" /></ac:link></li></ul><p><strong>Related procedures</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating AcV-1 diagram" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Building AcV-1 matrix" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

- space_key: `MT`

- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

413597688

413597690

413597689

**Description**

The AcV-2 view provides a timeline perspective on programmes.

The AcV-2 View is intended primarily to support the acquisition and fielding processes including the management of dependencies between projects and the integration of all the DLODs to achieve a successfully integrated military capability.

For capability-based procurement, these work streams might conveniently be equated with Defence Lines of Development (DLODs).

**Implementation**

AcV-2 can be represented using an AcV-2 diagram which is realized as a Gantt Chart.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Acv-2 Programme Timelines

**Related views**

The use of AcV-2 should support the management of capability delivery and be aligned with the capability phasing view (StV-3), if one exists.

413597687

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249562859 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 05693: MODAF. AV-1 Overview and Summary Information

- page_id: `249562859`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249562859/MODAF.+AV-1+Overview+and+Summary+Information
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-18T17:05:55.691+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > UAF 1.3 modeling > MODAF > MODAF All Views Viewpoint
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

411544091

411544094

411544092

**Description**

The overview and summary information contained within the AV-1 product provides an executive-level summary information in a consistent form that allows for quick reference and comparison between architectural descriptions. AV-1 includes assumptions, constraints, and limitations that may affect high-level decisions relating to an architecture. In an enterprise repository environment, individual architectures are mapped against enterprise phases to provide context between the architectures. AV-1 is usually a structured text product. Organizations may create a template for the AV-1 that can then be used to create a consistent set of information across different architecture-based projects.

**Implementation**

AV-1 can be represented:

- Using a report, which is automatically generated from all data. Since only a partial AV-1 report can be generated, the rest of data must be filled in manually.
- Using a AV-1 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Fragment of AV-1 Overview and Summary Information report

411544089

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Architectural Description' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Architecture Metadata' space='MED']

**Related procedures**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Managing reports' space='']

In an enterprise repository environment, individual architectures are mapped against enterprise phases to provide context between the architectures. AV-1 is usually a structured text product. Organizations may create a template for the AV-1 that can then be used to create a consistent set of information across different architecture-based projects.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>AV-1 can be represented:</p><ul><li>Using a report, which is automatically generated from all data. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249562862 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 05694: MODAF. AV-2 Integrated Dictionary

- page_id: `249562862`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249562862/MODAF.+AV-2+Integrated+Dictionary
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-18T17:05:55.857+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > UAF 1.3 modeling > MODAF > MODAF All Views Viewpoint
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

411641246

411641249

411641248

**Description**

An AV-2 presents all the Elements used in an architecture as a standalone structure. An AV-2 presents all the Elements as a specialization hierarchy, provides a text definition for each one and references the source of the element. An AV-2 shows elements from the Ontology that have been used in the architecture and new elements that have been introduced by the architecture. Architectures often introduce new terms – usually because the architecture is covering new technology or business processes. The purpose of the AV-2 is to provide a local extension of the Ontology to explain the terms and abbreviations used in building the architecture.

**Implementation**

AV-2 can be represented:

- Using a report, which is automatically generated from all data.
- An AV-2 table. If you have AV-2 diagrams from previous versions, they will be migrated into the AV-1 diagram when loading the project with UPDM 17.0.2.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### AV-2 Integrated Dictionary

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Fragment of AV-2 Integrated Dictionary report

411641245

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Definition' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Alias' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Same As' space='MED']

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Managing reports' space='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249562925 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 05695: MODAF. OV-1 High-Level Operational Concept Graphic

- page_id: `249562925`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249562925/MODAF.+OV-1+High-Level+Operational+Concept+Graphic
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-08-18T13:09:26.483+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > UAF 1.3 modeling > MODAF > MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

413963572

413963574

413963573

**Description**

The purpose of High-level Operational Concept Graphic is to provide a high-level graphical and textual description of operational concept (high level organizations, missions, geographic configuration, connectivity, etc) of what the architecture is supposed to do, and how it is supposed to do it. The OV-1, along with the corresponding AV-1 product is intended to serve as an executive summary of the architecture.

**Implementation**

OV-1 can be represented using:

- Link to an external document.
- OV-1 diagram which is based on the UML Composite Structure diagram.
- OV-1 Free Form diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.
- UML Composite Structure Diagram.

**Sample** 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### OV-1 High-Level Operational Concept Graphic

413963571

**Related elements**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='High Level Operational Concept' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Concept Role' space='MED']

**Related procedures**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating OV-1 diagram' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

The OV-1, along with the corresponding AV-1 product is intended to serve as an executive summary of the architecture.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>OV-1 can be represented using:</p><ul><li>Link to an external document.</li><li>OV-1 diagram which is based on the UML Composite Structure diagram.</li><li>OV-1 Free Form diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.</li><li>UML Composite Structure Diagram.</li></ul><p><strong>Sample</strong><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="OV_1.jpg" /></ac:image></p><h6>OV-1 High-Level Operational Concept Graphic</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="83312f6d-985d-4bf9-8c57-d6afd0ab57ec"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">413963571</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related elements</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="High Level Operational Concept" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Concept Role" /></ac:link></li></ul><p><strong>Related procedures</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating OV-1 diagram" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249562930 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 05696: MODAF. OV-2 Operational Node Internal Relationship Description

- page_id: `249562930`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249562930/MODAF.+OV-2+Operational+Node+Internal+Relationship+Description
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-08-18T13:09:26.610+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > UAF 1.3 modeling > MODAF > MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

414117534

414117536

414117535

**Implementation**

OV-2 can be represented using:

- OV-2 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.
- OV-2 diagram which is based on the UML Composite Structure diagram.
- UML Class diagram.
- UML Composite Structure diagram.
- SysML Block Definition diagram.
- SysML Internal Block diagram.

**Sample**

**[IMAGE alt='' src='']**

###### OV-2 Operational Node Internal Relationship Description

**Related views**

An OV-2 is highly related with an OV-5. Operational Nodes shown in the OV-2 are the performers of the Operational Activities modeled in the OV-5. OV-2 focuses on the Operational Nodes, with the activities being a secondary adornment. The OV-5, on the other hand, places first-order attention on operational activities and only second-order attention on Nodes, which can be shown as annotations or swim-lanes on the activities.

Information flows can be modeled either in the OV-2 or OV-5. In both cases they are highly associated and in general should be reused between these views.

The OV-2 displays the Capabilities required by Nodes from StV-2. That is an association between two abstraction levels of user requirements where the OV is more specific than the StV and a Node is more specific concept than a Capability.

The other important mapping is between OV-2 and SV-1. The specification Node and implementation Resource are subjects to map here. One OV-2 product can have several implementations in the SV-1.

414117533

**Related elements**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Known Resource' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Connector' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Port' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Role' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Exchange' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Measurement' space='MED']

**Related procedures**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with OV-2 diagram' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ba8bbe68-2ac2-4b9d-9ba4-f1263943931c"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">414117534</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2c04675f-c9e6-46ab-af74-640829af3c31"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">414117536</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="174aa228-60a6-4f60-a2a9-ad8605c091cf"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">414117535</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>OV-2 can be represented using:</p><ul><li>OV-2 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.</li><li>OV-2 diagram which is based on the UML Composite Structure diagram.</li><li>UML Class diagram.</li><li>UML Composite Structure diagram.</li><li>SysML Block Definition diagram.</li><li>SysML Internal Block diagram.</li></ul><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><strong><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Ov_2_internal.png" /></ac:image><br /></strong></p><h6>OV-2 Operational Node Internal Relationship Description</h6><p><strong>Related views</strong></p><p>An OV-2 is highly related with an OV-5. Operational Nodes shown in the OV-2 are the performers of the Operational Activities modeled in the OV-5. OV-2 focuses on the Operational Nodes, with the activities being a secondary adornment. The OV-5, on the other hand, places first-order attention on operational activities and only second-order attention on Nodes, which can be shown as annotations or swim-lanes on the activities.</p><p>Information flows can be modeled either in the OV-2 or OV-5. In both cases they are highly associated and in general should be reused between these views.</p><p>The OV-2 displays the Capabilities required by Nodes from StV-2. That is an association between two abstraction levels of user requirements where the OV is more specific than the StV and a Node is more specific concept than a Capability.</p><p>The other important mapping is between OV-2 and SV-1. The specification Node and implementation Resource are subjects to map here. One OV-2 product can have several implementations in the SV-1.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2fe065e4-a687-498f-a252-6d5be2019214"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">414117533</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related elements</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Known Resource" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Operational Connector" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Operational Port" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Operational Role" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Operational Exchange" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Measurement" /></ac:link></li></ul><p><strong>Related procedures</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Working with OV-2 diagram" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249562933 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 05697: MODAF. OV-2 Operational Node Relationship Description

- page_id: `249562933`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249562933/MODAF.+OV-2+Operational+Node+Relationship+Description
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-18T17:05:58.103+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > UAF 1.3 modeling > MODAF > MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

414271992

414271994

414271993

**Description**

The Operational Node Relationship Description tracks the need to exchange information from specific operational nodes (that play a key role in the architecture) to others. An OV-2 does not depict the connectivity between the nodes. MODAF modifies the OV-2 in two ways. First, it recommends that an OV-2 diagram (now OV2a) shows the platforms or geographic locations at which operational nodes are deployed. Secondly, it provides additional information (OV-2b) about each needline in the form of a requirements specification. There are now four types of needlines identified as follows:

1. InformationExchange
2. EnergyFlow
3. MaterielFlow
4. MovementOfPeople

In addition, MODAF permits service-oriented architectures. Instead of needlines between nodes, it is possible to show which services the nodes provide and consume. Finally, MODAF again permits known resources to be shown in an OV-2. However, this must be clearly shown as a KnownResource in an OV-2 model. LogicalArchitecture, which is the container class for all the nodes and KnownResources, is introduced.

**Implementation**

OV-2 can be represented using:

- OV-2 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.
- OV-2 diagram which is based on the UML Composite Structure diagram.
- UML Class diagram.
- UML Composite Structure diagram.
- SysML Block Definition diagram.
- SysML Internal Block diagram.

**Sample**

**[IMAGE alt='' src='']**

###### OV-2 Operational Node Relationship Description

**Related views**

An OV-2 is highly related with an OV-5. Operational Nodes shown in the OV-2 are the performers of the Operational Activities modeled in the OV-5. OV-2 focuses on the Operational Nodes, with the activities being a secondary adornment. The OV-5, on the other hand, places first-order attention on operational activities and only second-order attention on Nodes, which can be shown as annotations or swimlanes on the activities.

Information flows can be modeled either in the OV-2 or OV-5. In both cases they are highly associated and in general should be reused between these views.

The OV-2 displays the Capabilities required by Nodes from StV-2. That is an association between two abstraction levels of user requirements where the OV is more specific than the StV and a Node is more specific concept than a Capability.

The other important mapping is between OV-2 and SV-1. The specification Node and implementation Resource are subjects to map here. One OV-2 product can have several implementations in the SV-1.

414271991

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249562938 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 05698: MODAF. OV-3 Operational Information Exchange Matrix

- page_id: `249562938`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249562938/MODAF.+OV-3+Operational+Information+Exchange+Matrix
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-08-18T13:09:26.827+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > UAF 1.3 modeling > MODAF > MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

Information exchanges express the relationship across the three basic architecture data elements of an OV (Operational Activities, Operational Performers, and Information Flow) with a focus on the specific aspects of the Information Flow and the information content.

The Information Exchanges of the OV-3 should remain at a high level of aggregation to represent actual information workflow products that are used at the Operational Performers shown in the OV-2 (and not their subordinate Operational Performers).

**Implementation**

OV-3 can be represented using:

- OV-3 table.
- OV-3 role-based table. A role-based table represents exchanges between Operational Roles.
- OV-3 spreadsheet report.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### OV-3 Operational Information Exchange Matrix

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### OV-3 Role-based Operational Information Exchange Matrix

**Related views**

An OV-3 is initially constructed from the information contained in the Operational Node Relationship Description (OV-2).

**Related elements**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Performer' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Exchange' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Information' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Natural Resource' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Resource Artifact' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Post' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Software' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Capability Configuration' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Organization' space='MED']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249562945 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 05699: MODAF. OV-4 Organizational Relationships Chart

- page_id: `249562945`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249562945/MODAF.+OV-4+Organizational+Relationships+Chart
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-18T17:05:58.512+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > UAF 1.3 modeling > MODAF > MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

414660137

414660139

414660138

**Description**

The Organizational Relationships Chart illustrates the command structure or relationships (as opposed to relationships with respect to a business process flow) among human roles, organizations, or organization types that are the key players in architecture. MODAF divides The OV-4 in two views: an OV-4 Typical and an OV-4 Actual. The former is exactly as the DoDAF OV-4, while the latter is a special form of the SV-1; where the resources are restricted to being organizational.

**Implementation**

OV-4 can be represented using:

- OV-4 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.
- UML Class diagram.
- SysML Block Definition diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### OV-4 Organizational Relationships Chart

**Related views**

The organizations that are modeled using an OV-4 in the Operational Viewpoint may also appear in other views, for example, SV-1 (organizational constituents of a capability configuration), AcV-1 (actual organizations that own projects), and SV-5 (organizational resources performs functions).

414660136

**Related elements**

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249562950 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 05700: MODAF. OV-5 Operational Activity Model

- page_id: `249562950`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249562950/MODAF.+OV-5+Operational+Activity+Model
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-18T17:05:58.713+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > UAF 1.3 modeling > MODAF > MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

414777526

414777528

414777527

**Description**

The Operational Activity Model describes the operations that are normally conducted in the course of achieving a mission or a business goal, from a net-centric perspective. It describes capabilities, operational activities (or tasks), input and output (I/O) flows between activities, and I/O flows to/from activities that are outside the scope of the architecture. It is imperative that the levels-of-detail between the OV-2, OV-3, and OV-5 remain cohesive. For example, if one diagram of OV-2 operational nodes is developed that shows aggregated organizations only, then it is imperative that the corresponding OV-5 product be developed to show only those operational activities that are meaningful with respect to these operational nodes. Similarly, the information exchanges of OV-3 should remain at a high level of aggregation to represent actual information workflow products that are used at the operational nodes depicted in OV-2 (and not their subordinate operational nodes). The net-centric OV-5 may be used in the following ways:

- Delineate lines of dependency on external activities when coupled with an OV-2.
- Highlight information flows to depict the status of the information's refinement (raw, preprocessed, fused, etc.).
- Provide the critical foundation for depicting Task, Post, Process, and Use (TPPU) activity sequencing and timing in the OV-6a, OV-6b, and OV-6c.
- Identify critical mission threads and operational information exchanges by annotating which activities are critical, i.e., identify the activities in the model that are critical.

**Implementation**

OV-5 can be represented using:

- OV-5 diagram for Operational Activity hierarchies. This diagram is based on the UML Class diagram.
- OV-5 diagram for Operational Activity flows. This diagram is based on the UML Activity diagram.
- UML Class diagram.
- UML Activity diagram.
- SysML Block diagram.
- SysML Activity diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### OV-5 Operational Activity hierarchy Model

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Fragment of OV-5 Operational Activity Model

**Related views**

The OV-5 and OV-2 are, to a degree, complements of each other. An OV-5 focuses on the operational activities whereas OV-2 focuses on the operational nodes. Due to the relationship between nodes and operational activities, these types of view should normally be developed together.

To maintain this independence from implementation, the logical Nodes in an OV-2 are used to represent the structure which carries out the Operational Activities. Operational Activities are realized as Functions (SV-4) which are the “how” to the Operational Activities’ “what”, for example, they are specified in terms of the resources that carry them out.

The activities described in an OV-5 may be Standard Operational Activities which are defined in the StV-6 (which also maps the activities to corresponding capabilities).

414777524

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0635acf7-bdb5-4882-b75b-83f136eddfe1"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">414777526</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="971c5f7c-1c60-4c78-92bc-077efb2649b2"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">414777528</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6fc57138-7a0a-41e0-89c3-e60e66c5b5ce"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">414777527</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>The Operational Activity Model describes the operations that are normally conducted in the course of achieving a mission or a business goal, from a net-centric perspective. It describes capabilities, operational activities (or tasks), input and output (I/O) flows between activities, and I/O flows to/from activities that are outside the scope of the architecture. It is imperative that the levels-of-detail between the OV-2, OV-3, and OV-5 remain cohesive. For example, if one diagram of OV-2 operational nodes is developed that shows aggregated organizations only, then it is imperative that the corresponding OV-5 product be developed to show only those operational activities that are meaningful with respect to these operational nodes. Similarly, the information exchanges of OV-3 should remain at a high level of aggregation to represent actual information workflow products that are used at the operational nodes depicted in OV-2 (and not their subordinate operational nodes). The net-centric OV-5 may be used in the following ways:</p><ul><li>Delineate lines of dependency on external activities when coupled with an OV-2.</li><li>Highlight information flows to depict the status of the information's refinement (raw, preprocessed, fused, etc.).</li><li>Provide the critical foundation for depicting Task, Post, Process, and Use (TPPU) activity sequencing and timing in the OV-6a, OV-6b, and OV-6c.</li><li>Identify critical mission threads and operational information exchanges by annotating which activities are critical, i.e., identify the activities in the model that are critical.</li></ul><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>OV-5 can be represented using:</p><ul><li>OV-5 diagram for Operational Activity hierarchies. This diagram is based on the UML Class diagram.</li><li>OV-5 diagram for Operational Activity flows. This diagram is based on the UML Activity diagram.</li><li>UML Class diagram.</li><li>UML Activity diagram.</li><li>SysML Block diagram.</li><li>SysML Activity diagram.</li></ul><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Ov_5.jpg" /></ac:image></p><h6>OV-5 Operational Activity hierarchy Model</h6><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Ov_5_example.jpg" /></ac:image></p><h6>Fragment of OV-5 Operational Activity Model</h6><p><strong>Related views</strong></p><p>The OV-5 and OV-2 are, to a degree, complements of each other. An OV-5 focuses on the operational activities whereas OV-2 focuses on the operational nodes. Due to the relationship between nodes and operational activities, these types of view should normally be developed together. </p><p>To maintain this independence from implementation, the logical Nodes in an OV-2 are used to represent the structure which carries out the Operational Activities. Operational Activities are realized as Functions (SV-4) which are the “how” to the Operational Activities’ “what”, for example, they are specified in terms of the resources that carry them out.</p><p>The activities described in an OV-5 may be Standard Operational Activities which are defined in the StV-6 (which also maps the activities to corresponding capabilities).</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="57d993ee-90ab-4602-9e83-aeac83563655"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">414777524</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related elements</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Operational Activity" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Operational Parameter" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Standard Operational Activity" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Operational Performer" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Is Capable To Perform" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Operational Activity Action" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Operational Activity Edge" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Operational Exchange" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Operational Information" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Natural Resource" /></ac:link></li></ul><p><strong>Related procedures</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating OV-5 Operational Activity Model diagram" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating OV-5 Operational Activity Flow Model diagram" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Displaying possible Operational Exchanges on the selected Operational Activity Edge" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating Process Flow Diagrams From Compositions or Aggregations Defined in Process Diagrams" /></ac:link></li></ul><p><strong>Related references</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Realized Operational Exchange wizard" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Operational Exchange Manager dialog" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249562960 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 05701: MODAF. OV-6a Operational Rules Model

- page_id: `249562960`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249562960/MODAF.+OV-6a+Operational+Rules+Model
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-08-18T13:09:27.562+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > UAF 1.3 modeling > MODAF > MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

414963391

414963393

414963392

**Description**

The Operational Rules Model specifies operational or business rules that are constraints on an enterprise, a mission, operation, business, or an architecture. While other OV views (e.g., OV-1, OV-2, and OV-5) describe the structure of a business—what the business can do—for the most part, they do not describe what the business must do, or what it cannot do. At the mission level, an OV-6a may consist of doctrine, guidance, rules of engagement, and so forth. At the operation level, rules may include such things as a military Operational Plan (OPLAN). At lower levels, an OV-6a describes the rules under which the architecture or its nodes behave under specified conditions. Such rules can be expressed in a textual form, for example, “If (these conditions) exist, and (this event) occurs, then (perform these actions).” At a top level, rules should at least embody the concepts of operations defined in an OV-1, and should provide guidelines for the development and definition of more detailed 
rules and behavioral definitions that will occur later in the architecture definition process.

**Implementation**

OV-6a can be represented using:

- OV-6a table.
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='OV-6a Operational Node Parametric' space=''] .
- OV-6a spreadsheet report.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### OV-6a Operational Rules Model

**Related views**

An OV-6a constrains the structure elements of OV-1, OV-2, and OV-5. OV-6a can also be used to extend the capture of business requirements by constraining the structure and validity of the OV-7 elements.

As the View name implies, the rules captured in an OV-6a are operational (i.e., mission-oriented) whereas resource-oriented rules are defined in an SV-10 (OV-6 is the “what” to SV-10’s “how”).

**Constraint owner**

You can choose where all the constraints will be stored through the**Project Options**.

To select a constraint owner

1. In the main menu, click Options > Project . The Project Options dialog opens.
2. Go to General > UAF , and in the Default Constraint Owners property group, choose a needed owner.

414963390

**Related elements**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Constraint' space='MED']

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Activity' space='MED']

While other OV views (e.g., OV-1, OV-2, and OV-5) describe the structure of a business—what the business can do—for the most part, they do not describe what the business must do, or what it cannot do. At lower levels, an OV-6a describes the rules under which the architecture or its nodes behave under specified conditions. Such rules can be expressed in a textual form, for example, “If (these conditions) exist, and (this event) occurs, then (perform these actions).” At a top level, rules should at least embody the concepts of operations defined in an OV-1, and should provide guidelines for the development and definition of more detailed<br />rules and behavioral definitions that will occur later in the architecture definition process.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>OV-6a can be represented using:</p><ul><li>OV-6a table.</li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="OV-6a Operational Node Parametric" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[OV-6a Operational Node Parametric diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li><li>OV-6a spreadsheet report.</li></ul><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Ov_6a.jpg" /></ac:image></p><h6>OV-6a Operational Rules Model</h6><p><strong>Related views</strong></p><p>An OV-6a constrains the structure elements of OV-1, OV-2, and OV-5. OV-6a can also be used to extend the capture of business requirements by constraining the structure and validity of the OV-7 elements.</p><p>As the View name implies, the rules captured in an OV-6a are operational (i.e., mission-oriented) whereas resource-oriented rules are defined in an SV-10 (OV-6 is the “what” to SV-10’s “how”).</p><p style=""><strong>Constraint owner</strong></p><p style="">You can choose where all the constraints will be stored through the<span> </span><strong>Project Options</strong>.</p><p style="">To select a constraint owner</p><hr style="" /><ol style=""><li>In the main menu, click<span> </span><strong>Options</strong><span> </span>&gt;<span> </span><strong>Project</strong>. The<span> </span><strong>Project Options</strong><span> </span>dialog opens.</li><li>Go to<span> </span><strong>General</strong><span> </span>&gt;<span> </span><strong>UAF</strong>, and in the<span> </span><strong>Default Constraint Owners</strong><span> </span>property group, choose a needed owner.</li></ol></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="454d05a9-9e7f-4f58-8b1b-b027f18eaa7d"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">414963390</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related elements</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Operational Constraint" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Operational Performer" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Operational Activity" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Operational Exchange" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Operational Information" /></ac:link></li></ul><p><strong>Related procedures</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Working with OV-6a table" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249562967 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 05702: MODAF. OV-6b Operational State Transition Description

- page_id: `249562967`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249562967/MODAF.+OV-6b+Operational+State+Transition+Description
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-18T17:05:59.195+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > UAF 1.3 modeling > MODAF > MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

415053735

415053738

415053736

**Description**

The Operational State Transition Description is a graphical method of describing how an operational node or activity responds to various events by changing its state. The diagram represents the sets of events to which the architecture will respond (by taking an action to move to a new state) as a function of its current state. Each transition specifies an event and an action. The explicit sequencing of activities in response to external and internal events is not fully expressed in an OV-5. An OV-6b can be used to describe the explicit sequencing of the operational activities.

Alternatively, an OV-6b can be used to reflect the explicit sequencing of actions internal to a single operational activity or the sequencing of operational activities with respect to a specific operational node. In a net-centric architecture, the OV-6b is used to describe the set of state transitions for providers and consumers in the NetCentric Environment (NCE) in response to the posting of information to the NCE or retrieving of information from the NCE.

**Implementation**

OV-6b can be represented using a UML State Machine diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### OV-6b Operational State Transition Description

**Related views**

An OV-6b can be used to describe the detailed sequencing of activities or work flow in the business process. The OV-6b is particularly useful for describing critical sequencing of behaviors and timing of operational activities that cannot be adequately described in the Activity Model (OV-5).

415053734

**Related elements**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational State Description' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational States' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Performer' space='MED']

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Allocate Activities to State Internal Behaviors' space='']

The diagram represents the sets of events to which the architecture will respond (by taking an action to move to a new state) as a function of its current state. Each transition specifies an event and an action. The explicit sequencing of activities in response to external and internal events is not fully expressed in an OV-5. An OV-6b can be used to describe the explicit sequencing of the operational activities.</p><p>Alternatively, an OV-6b can be used to reflect the explicit sequencing of actions internal to a single operational activity or the sequencing of operational activities with respect to a specific operational node. In a net-centric architecture, the OV-6b is used to describe the set of state transitions for providers and consumers in the NetCentric Environment (NCE) in response to the posting of information to the NCE or retrieving of information from the NCE.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>OV-6b can be represented using a UML State Machine diagram.</p><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Ov_6b.jpg" /></ac:image></p><h6>OV-6b Operational State Transition Description</h6><p><strong>Related views</strong></p><p>An OV-6b can be used to describe the detailed sequencing of activities or work flow in the business process. The OV-6b is particularly useful for describing critical sequencing of behaviors and timing of operational activities that cannot be adequately described in the Activity Model (OV-5).</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="57f8294b-50b4-48ab-bdb2-8b31cfff3677"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">415053734</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related elements</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Operational State Description" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Operational States" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Operational Performer" /></ac:link></li></ul><p><strong>Related procedures</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Allocate Activities to State Internal Behaviors" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249562970 space=MT version=3 -->
## PAGE 05703: MODAF. OV-6c Operational Event-Trace Description

- page_id: `249562970`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249562970/MODAF.+OV-6c+Operational+Event-Trace+Description
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-09-12T13:40:22.157+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > UAF 1.3 modeling > MODAF > MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

415390576

415390578

415390577

**Description**

The Operational Event-Trace Description provides a time-ordered examination of the information exchanges between participating operational nodes as a result of a particular operational thread or scenario. Each event-trace diagram should have an accompanying description that defines the particular scenario or situation and represent a specific capability. The OV-6c is also used in conjunction with an OV-5 to depict process flow (such as an IDEF3 model). A process flow model captures precedence and causality relations between situations and events by providing a structured method for expressing knowledge about how a process or organization works. A process flow model should be annotated with the names of the operational nodes responsible for conducting those activities.

The net-centric OV-6c describes the business and mission processes that need to be executed to achieve NetCentric Operations (NCO). The ability to discover, access, and understand information and capabilities from the NCE,where and when they are needed, is supported by the OV-6c and can be decomposed to the level of specificity required for the subject architecture. In the NCE, the OV-6c may depict the following:

- Exchanges between the Service Functionality Providers and Service Consumers, the Service Consumers and external Service Functionality Providers, and between the Service Functionality Providers and Unanticipated Users.
- Sequences that describe the timeline for the availability of information for any of its refinement states (raw, preprocessed, fused, etc.).
- Handling, methodologies, and the Enterprise Information Environment (EIE) infrastructure components that support the operational concepts of post before processing.
- Illustration of one-to-many, many-to-one, and many-to-many exchanges between Service Functionality Providers and Service Consumers found in the net-centric OV-3.

**Implementation**

OV-6c can be represented using a UML Sequence diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### OV-6c Operational Event-Trace Description

**Related views**

The OV-6c can be used by itself or in conjunction with an Operational State Transition Description (OV-6b) to describe the dynamic behavior of processes.

The information content of messages that connect life-lines in an OV-6c View Product may be related, in modeling terms, with the information flows (OV-3, OV-5) and information entities (OV-7) modeled in other views.

415390575

**Related elements**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Interaction' space='MMA']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Message' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Exchange' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Performer' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Role' space='MED']

**Related references**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Realized Operational Exchange wizard' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Exchange Manager dialog' space='']

Each event-trace diagram should have an accompanying description that defines the particular scenario or situation and represent a specific capability. The OV-6c is also used in conjunction with an OV-5 to depict process flow (such as an IDEF3 model). A process flow model captures precedence and causality relations between situations and events by providing a structured method for expressing knowledge about how a process or organization works. A process flow model should be annotated with the names of the operational nodes responsible for conducting those activities.</p><p>The net-centric OV-6c describes the business and mission processes that need to be executed to achieve NetCentric Operations (NCO). The ability to discover, access, and understand information and capabilities from the NCE,where and when they are needed, is supported by the OV-6c and can be decomposed to the level of specificity required for the subject architecture. In the NCE, the OV-6c may depict the following: </p><ul><li>Exchanges between the Service Functionality Providers and Service Consumers, the Service Consumers and external Service Functionality Providers, and between the Service Functionality Providers and Unanticipated Users.</li><li>Sequences that describe the timeline for the availability of information for any of its refinement states (raw, preprocessed, fused, etc.).</li><li>Handling, methodologies, and the Enterprise Information Environment (EIE) infrastructure components that support the operational concepts of post before
